title: PutObject, Metadata, Checksums, and Encryption Defaults · Execution Trace: Server Encryption Default
summary: A service uploads an unbounded reader, uses the returned ETag as a universal digest, loses content metadata on replacement, and cannot prove which bytes S3 accepted. Predict the representation, storage, trust, cost, and recovery evidence before inspecting a model. Planned artifact: Add and verify the server encryption default increment in single-object upload and integrity evidence record. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal debugging, and eviden…
objectives: Verify S3 default encryption and choose SSE-S3 or SSE-KMS requirements without claiming transport or client-side confidentiality.
Set and preserve Content-Type, Content-Disposition, Cache-Control, encoding, language, custom metadata, and tags during replacements and copies.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

steps.title: S3, CloudFront, and Go Predict Server Encryption Default · Theory Server Encryption Default
steps.instruction:
theory case — A theory Go file-delivery team handles case 6727 during a compliance restore drill. Build deterministic Go evidence to prove accepted bytes, checksum, metadata, and encryption without using ETag as a universal digest; cap bytes, time, attempts, concurrency, and spend before starting work; then change region, range, or signed header and explain the exact failure boundary. Browser code uses pure values and state transitions only. AWS SDK packages, networks, S3, CloudFront, KMS, IAM, WAF, databases, credentials, host state, race and leak checks, load, faults, restore, billing, and production behavior require explicit authorized transfer gates.

Before reading, predict which action preserves the Server Encryption Default contract for the supplied data.
